items.foco: Red flags in low back pain
items.title: Red flags in low back pain
items.contentHtml:
Red flags in low back pain

  • Red flags include: age >50 years, previous cancer, unexplained weight loss, fever, bacterial infection, immunosuppression, major trauma, nocturnal pain that awakens, progressive neurological deficit or cauda equina syndrome.
  • The presence of a red flag requires urgent imaging. (nuclear magnetic resonance) and rule out secondary causes such as vertebral metastasis, epidural abscess or pathological fracture.
  • In elderly people with osteoporosis, suspect compression fracture if sudden pain after minimal effort; Plain radiography may show vertebral wedging.

items.foco: Mechanical vs inflammatory low back pain
items.title: Mechanical vs inflammatory low back pain
items.contentHtml:
Mechanical vs inflammatory low back pain

  • Mechanical low back pain: worsens with activity, improves with rest, gradual onset, no prolonged morning stiffness; Most common cause: muscular or ligamentous overload.
  • Inflammatory low back pain: improves with exercise, worsens with rest, morning stiffness >30 minutes, insidious onset before age 40; typical of spondyloarthritis such as ankylosing spondylitis.
  • Criteria for inflammatory low back pain (ASAS): at least 4 of: age at onset <40 years, insidious onset, improvement with exercise, no improvement with rest, nocturnal pain (with awakening).

items.title: Lasègue sign and maneuvers
items.contentHtml:
Lasègue sign and maneuvers

  • Lasègue sign (straight leg raising maneuver): positive if radicular (sciatic) pain appears between 30° and 70° of hip flexion with the knee extended; indicates root irritation (L4-S1).
  • Crossed lasègue: pain in the symptomatic leg when raising the asymptomatic leg; greater specificity for herniated disc.
  • Bragard maneuver: dorsiflexion of the foot at the end of the Lasègue; intensifies radicular pain, confirms positivity.
  • Wassermann maneuver (inverted Lasègue): pain in the anterior aspect of the thigh suggests involvement of the L2-L3-L4 root.

items.foco: Most common cause of low back pain
items.title: Most common cause of low back pain
items.contentHtml:
Most common cause of low back pain

  • The most common cause of acute low back pain is nonspecific mechanical (90% of cases), related to muscle or ligament strain, without root involvement or serious structural pathology.
  • The lumbar disc herniation is the most common cause of low back pain with radiculopathy (sciatica), especially at the L4-L5 level and L5-S1.
  • Other common causes: spondylolisthesis, lumbar canal stenosis and compression fracture in the elderly.

items.title: Initial treatment with NSAIDs
items.contentHtml:
Initial treatment with NSAIDs

  • The initial treatment of acute non-specific mechanical low back pain includes non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) as the first line of pharmacology, for example ibuprofen 400-600 mg every 8 hours or naproxen 500 mg every 12 hours, for 1-2 weeks.
  • Yes contraindication or intolerance to NSAIDs, use paracetamol (maximum 3 g/day) or muscle relaxants such as cyclobenzaprine 5-10 mg every 8 hours for short duration.
  • Routine opioids are not recommended; reserve for severe uncontrolled pain, for the short term and with monitoring.

items.title: Cauda equina syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Equina tail syndrome

  • Equina tail syndrome is a neurosurgical emergency due to compression of the lumbar and sacral roots distal to the conus medullaris.
  • Clinic: saddle anesthesia (loss of sensitivity in the perineal region), urinary retention with overflow incontinence, motor deficit in the lower extremities (paresis) and achilles areflexia.
  • If suspected, request urgent magnetic resonance imaging and refer to decompressive surgery in <24-48 hours to avoid permanent damage.

items.title: Indicated imaging study (MRI)
items.contentHtml:
Indicated imaging study (MRI)

  • Nuclear magnetic resonance imaging is the study of choice to evaluate low back pain with red flags, suspected disc herniation, canal stenosis, infection or neoplasia.
  • It is not routinely indicated in acute non-specific mechanical low back pain without warning signs; reserved for persistent pain >6 weeks with radiculopathy or suspected structural pathology.
  • Plain x-ray may be used initially if fracture, spondylolisthesis, or scoliosis is suspected; computed tomography is an alternative if contraindication to nuclear magnetic resonance.

items.title: Lumbar disc herniation and radiculopathy
items.contentHtml:
Lumbar disc herniation and radiculopathy

  • Lumbar disc herniation: extrusion of the nucleus pulposus through the annulus fibrosus, more common at L4-L5 and L5-S1.
  • Radiculopathy due to L4-L5 herniation affects the L5 root: weakness of the extensor hallucis longus and dorsiflexion of the foot, hypoesthesia in the dorsum of the foot and first toe.
  • L5-S1 hernia affects S1 root: weakness of plantar flexion (calfs), hypoesthesia in the lateral edge of the foot and heel, achillian areflexia.
  • Initial treatment is conservative (NSAIDs, physiotherapy); Surgery indicated if progressive motor deficit or cauda equina syndrome.

items.foco: Chronic low back pain and exercise
items.title: Chronic low back pain and exercise
items.contentHtml:
Chronic low back pain and exercise

  • Chronic low back pain (>12 weeks) requires a multidisciplinary approach: therapeutic exercise, cognitive-behavioral therapy and education.
  • aerobic exercise and strengthening of the paravertebral and abdominal muscles (core) are the first line; pilates or McKenzie method programs show benefit.
  • Avoid prolonged rest; It is recommended to maintain activity within tolerable limits. NSAIDs or antidepressants (duloxetine) can be used as adjuvants.

items.title: Narrow lumbar canal
items.contentHtml:
Narrow lumbar canal

  • Lumbar canal stenosis: narrowing of the spinal canal that compresses the roots, more common in the elderly due to facet and ligament hypertrophy.
  • Clinic: neurogenic claudication (pain, paresthesias and weakness in the legs when walking, which is relieved by sitting or flexing the trunk); difference from vascular claudication (relieves when stopping without flexion).
  • Nuclear magnetic resonance confirms the diagnosis; Conservative initial treatment (physiotherapy, NSAIDs), and decompressive surgery if it fails or there is progressive deficit.

items.title: Osteoporosis fracture in the elderly
items.contentHtml:
Osteoporotic fracture in the elderly

  • Osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture is a frequent cause of acute low back pain in the elderly, after minimal or spontaneous trauma.
  • Clinic: sudden, intense low back pain, located in the area of ​​the fracture, with functional limitation; may radiate to flanks.
  • Diagnosis: simple x-ray shows vertebral wedging; nuclear magnetic resonance differentiates acute from chronic fracture and rules out malignant pathology.
  • Treatment: analgesia (NSAIDs, paracetamol), short rest, calcitonin or bisphosphonates; consider vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty if refractory pain.

items.title: Acute, subacute and chronic duration
items.contentHtml:
Acute, subacute and chronic duration

  • Acute low back pain: duration <4 weeks; the majority self-limited, symptomatic management with NSAIDs and education.
  • Subacute low back pain: between 4 and 12 weeks; requires reassessing causes and optimizing conservative treatment, including physiotherapy.
  • Chronic low back pain: >12 weeks; multidisciplinary approach with exercise, cognitive-behavioral therapy and chronic pain control; avoid long-term opioids.
  • Persistence beyond 4-6 weeks with radiculopathy or red flags warrants imaging with nuclear magnetic resonance.
